mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers prayer across religious traditions including contemplative, petitionary, and intercessory forms, communal versus private prayer, and liturgy — a precise match to rituals of worship: prayer (A.1.a).

Covers the history of marriage as a religious institution including its Christianisation as a sacrament, arranged marriage, and ceremonial customs — directly relevant to passage rites including marriage (A.2).
Covers death rituals from ancient Egyptian and Greek funerary practices through Christian ars moriendi to modern hospice movement — directly relevant to passage rites concerning death (A.2).

The Franciscans and Dominicans as mendicant orders exemplify monasticism (A.3.c), forms of religious organization (B.2), and institutions of religious education through their role in medieval universities (B.4).

The Oxford Movement's revival of liturgical practices, sacramental theology, and its influence on church architecture and worship directly addresses forms of religious organization (B.2) and ceremonial and religious objects/sacraments (C.3).
Biblical typology as an interpretive method applied to sacred writings (C.1) and its influence on religious art and iconography (C.2) make it relevant to material manifestations of religious beliefs.
Covers prophecy across Abrahamic religions and the distinction between prophecy and divination — directly relevant to prophecy and divination as a religious practice (A.3.f).

Purgatory's doctrine shaped medieval worship through prayers and indulgences for souls (A.1) and involved sacramental practices (C.3) including religious objects and ceremonies related to the afterlife.

mappings.episodeTitle: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
mappings.rationaleAI: The Egyptian Book of the Dead represents sacred writings (C.1), religious art and iconography (C.2), and funerary passage rites concerning death (A.2) in ancient Egyptian religion.
The Dissolution of the Monasteries directly addresses monasticism (A.3.c) and the destruction of religious institutional forms including religious houses, libraries, and art (B.2).

Lakshmi's worship during Diwali represents a religious feast/festival (A.4), and her iconography including lotus, gold coins, and temple representations exemplifies religious symbolism and iconography (C.2).

mappings.episodeTitle: Bruegel's The Fight Between Carnival and Lent
mappings.rationaleAI: Bruegel's painting depicts the contrast between Carnival and Lenten religious practices including feasts and festivals (A.4) and religious customs, providing visual documentation of religious iconography (C.2).

Shinto covers rituals and shrines (A.1, B.2), the concept of kami, and Shinto's continuing presence in Japanese cultural and religious life including ceremonial practices and religious symbolism (C.2).
Simone Weil's mystical experiences, her ascetic self-imposed starvation, and her attraction to Christianity while remaining outside the Church exemplify asceticism (A.3.e) as a religious practice.
John Wesley and Methodism covers the organisational structure of Methodist societies and classes (B.2) and Methodism's social and educational impact (B.4).

George Herbert's devotional poetry in The Temple exemplifies the expression of religious faith through art (C.2) and his pastoral ministry connects to religious dress and vestments of the Church of England clergy (C.4).
The Delphic Oracle covers methods of divination through the Pythia (A.3.f) and the sanctuary's religious institutional structure (B.2).
Kali's iconography (garland of skulls, dark skin) and worship in Tantric traditions exemplify religious symbolism and iconography (C.2), while her worship connects to sacrifice (A.1.d).

Thomas Becket's conflict between church and state authority, his canonisation, and the Canterbury pilgrimage cult address the relationship between religious and political institutions (B.1) and sainthood (B.3).
Weber's concept of charismatic authority including religious prophets and the routinisation of charisma into institutional structures is relevant to religious leadership and theocracies (B.1).
The Safavid Dynasty established Shia Islam as the state religion representing a theocratic element (B.1), with artistic flourishing in religious art and architecture (C.2).
